title: Subtle things that you realized about NGE on deep analysis
display_title: Subtle things that you realized about NGE on deep analysis
wikitext:
[]: Subtle things that you realized about NGE on deep analysis             Quote Post by Vegeta 20XX » Wed Aug 05, 2009 12:18 am Basically, what things make you go "I just now noticed that" or "you know, that could also mean..." and other things in or about the series (including the cast and everything) that are fairly unnoticeable until you look closely. For example: After looking a little at the Evageeks commentary, I found out that Akuma just so happens to be Commander A (and the announcer) .

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Aug 05, 2009 12:28 am Good grief... I'd say the vast majority of what I know about NGE only came through careful analysis, discussion, and study of what had already been written about it. Some of the most enlightening were things like the hand, train, and glasses motif, the Walls of Jericho interpretation... Perhaps my own personal best came with interpreting Gendo's hushed line to Ritsuko in NGE and the interpretation I formed based on what I called Anno's grandly diffused dichotomic motif which relates to just about every motif noted in NGE.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Wed Aug 05, 2009 12:29 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: Good grief... I'd say the vast majority of what I know about NGE only came through careful analysis, discussion, and study of what had already been written about it. Some of the most enlightening were things like the hand, train, and glasses motif, the Walls of Jericho interpretation... Perhaps my own personal best came with interpreting Gendo's hushed line to Ritsuko in NGE and the interpretation I formed based on what I called Anno's grandly diffused dichotomic motif which relates to just about every motif noted in NGE.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Aug 05, 2009 12:45 am Monk Ed wrote: What's this about Gendo's hushed line?!

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Wed Aug 05, 2009 1:28 am ... ... ... ...Ohhh myyy Go'ood. It all just clicked in this massive expanding explosion of understanding in my head. That is the one most perfect line ever for that situation... The weight it carries is unsurpassed. To think it was a line Gendo actually said before... I never would have thought of that! It ties everything about his relationship with Ritsuko together: it communicates the similarity of her fate to her mother's, and ... Jesus, to think Ritsuko even says "Liar" after it just like Naoko does! I can't believe nobody thought of taking that as the ultimate clue before! I'm 100% convinced. Jesus Christ, dude, you solved the biggest mystery that had ever loomed in anyone's head about EoE. It's just too freakin' perfect to not be true. ^To you and whoever else figured it out independently. ...The fact that the line starts with "I truly", and then the rest is silenced out, only further convinces me. Both are techniques indicating a restatement of something that had previously been said (like how, in a list, ellipses represent a repetition of information). Only the part that wasn't old information remained unsilenced (the "I truly"). And then of course, obviously, the fact that he's saying again to Ritsuko something he told her mother, only more emphatically... God damn. I'm just totally floored.

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Wed Aug 05, 2009 1:34 am First time I heard of it: http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?p=119561#119561 In this forum: http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?p=61089#61089 This topic seems like it'll quickly spiral into a mess of random discussion, it's best to take the discussion to the existing threads.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Aug 05, 2009 1:50 am Monk Ed wrote: I'm 100% convinced. It's just too freakin' perfect to not be true.

[]:             Quote Post by skikes » Wed Aug 05, 2009 9:07 am everything! i hadnt a f*cking clue what was going on until i typed evangelion into google! i mean, have u ever watched it with someone who has never heard it... i make every1 i know watch and its exhausting! Ill never forget the "WTFUCKINGFUCK" look on my best friends face when all of a sudden there was a fish tank filled full of reis!

[]:             Quote Post by Vegeta 20XX » Wed Aug 05, 2009 2:31 pm Err, that's kinda big for a "subtle things" topic.

[]:             Quote Post by Joseph the PRPD » Wed Aug 05, 2009 5:24 pm That Pen-Pen is God/FAR.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Wed Aug 05, 2009 5:30 pm I think just about everything I didn't notice, I eventually found out here on Evageeks. And after I found out, I went back and watched it again and was able to see it. Sometimes when I'm watching, I pause when it's showing random images really fast or when it shows a newspaper or something that goes by too fast to read. I got a lot out of that, because one in particular talking about Seele and Second Impact, IIRC.

[]:             Quote Post by Combs » Wed Aug 05, 2009 7:33 pm Some subtle things I've noticed, I was watching episode 1 again for the fourth time, with my mother :$ and I noticed the AT field blur that you see in episodes 19, (when Yui-sama raises her hand), 23, (when Unit-01 is sortied to fight Armisael), and 24 (during the fight between units 01 and 02 in terminal dogma) when Shinji's entry plug fills up with LCL for the first time. I also notice how meets Shinji for the first time on the shore, he says Shinji has a heart delicate like glass. Misato also mentions in the english dub that it's "Kaworu of the seashore". Sand can be found on the seashore, and when you melt sand together, you can get glass. It all fits together rather nicely.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Wed Aug 05, 2009 7:34 pm Combs wrote: Some subtle things I've noticed, I was watching episode 1 again for the fourth time, with my mother :$ and I noticed the AT field blur that you see in episodes 19, (when Yui-sama raises her hand), 23, (when Unit-01 is sortied to fight Armisael), and 24 (during the fight between units 01 and 02 in terminal dogma) when Shinji's entry plug fills up with LCL for the first time.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Aug 06, 2009 5:34 am The ATF blur is all over the place. Once you know about it, those blurs start popping out of the show like chestbursters out of colonists.

[]:             Quote Post by BC Baron » Sun Aug 23, 2009 3:49 am Hi There, I don't know whether one of my quasi-theories qualify for this thread, or if it even makes any sense, but I came up with it on my own after several viewings, so here goes. The first one has a tangential connection to Eva Yojimbo's comments, as it also involves the baggage that exists between Ritsuko and her mother. I recently re-watched the series and noticed something while I was sitting through episode 13, "Lilliputian Hitcher". Ritsuko was utilizing Magi-3 Casper, the one which is supposed to reflect the 'woman' aspect of Naoko Akagi (via personality transplant?), to destroy the eleventh angel which has already hacked into the other two Magi supercomputers. Now I should briefly mention that I'm a fan of the dub, so I'm not sure whether variations from the original script may have colored my viewpoint on this, but it occurred to me that Ritsuko may already know the exact circumstances of her mother's death and the reasons behind it (rejection from Gendo/guilt over Rei I). I don't think it's ever explicitly stated, but if you believe that Ritsuko knows all the details concerning her mother's suicide and that she is now programming some kind of evolutionary dead-end via Casper in order to defeat Ireul (which has more or less fused with what can, symbolically, be considered her mother)... Well, I don't have any kind of technical description of what she did or the programming "back doors" implemented, but I guess what I'm suggesting is that she basically used her inside information of both her mother's own demise and the Magi itself against the angel/her mother to recreate a digital version of Naoko's suicide and convince it to kill itself. Of course, it was all done to save the world and everything, but still - that's pretty cold. I feel that even extra emphasis is placed on this act when you realize that Casper (containing Naoko's womanly aspect) is the tool used by Ritsuko. Plus, at the end of the episode she comes right out and tells Misato how she hated that part of her mother's personality. She then goes on to mention that her mother remained a woman until the very end. I'm pretty sure the irony of those statements was lost on Misato just as it was lost on me the first couple of times I watched the series. Anyways, there it is. I don't know whether it counts as subtle, but since I personally didn't make this connection until after multiple viewings and analysis, I thought I'd post it here. Unfortunately, one of the problems I've had over the years with not having a lot of people around who are both A)familiar with Eva and B)willing to discuss it at great length, is that I'm never really sure whether any of my conclusions are bona fide revelations or just really obvious to the average viewer.

[]:             Quote Post by Evafan » Sun Aug 23, 2009 3:30 pm I never really noticed it, but I looked back on Kaji a little while ago. I never gave it much thought, but upon closer inspection I sort of got the vibe that Kaji had a father/son relationship going on with Shinji. Sure they didn't get that much time together on screen to concretely put it in your face, but I think it's there, and it's subtle. Misato I always saw as a mother figure to Shinji inb4 kiss in EoE. So Misato as a mother figure, and Kaji as a father figure was a fun coincidence given their relationship. Or maybe it was intentional, I think it was, but maybe that's just me fanwanking.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Mon Aug 24, 2009 12:30 am Obviously the first thing I noticed was the Judo-Christian imagery. It kinda confused me at first because I'm a Christian, so I knew very well what those symbols meant to Christianity. But they ended up meaning different things in Eva. It's all good, though. I'd rather it mean something different so they can tell their own story rather than try to shoe-horn Christ into Eva some how. The other thing I casually noticed during the first viewing was Anno's commentary on masks and how he carried that into a visual motif with clothing. The clothing the character's wear represented a "mask" or a wall that they were putting on for others (later it would be called the "AT Field"). Since the clothing represented the characters' "AT Field", the few times when you see the characters' true thought come out was when they were without clothing. Anything else I picked up on after that was on the second viewing, careful scrutiny and some internet research, particularly into Jewish mythology. After that, I realized where Eva gets it's analysis of women from Shinji's perspective. "The Mythology of Evangelion" gave me an interesting view of the series (it's all in Shinji's head and helps him deal with real life), although I don't know if it's one that I particularly fits with EVERYTHING in the series (instrumentality), although it is compatible with some of the elements in the series (the "dance" in episode 9 being symbolic of sex, followed by the discovery and destruction of an embryo on the part of the female in episode 10).

[]:             Quote Post by Teague » Mon Aug 24, 2009 7:50 am Something I never noticed at first was just how bad a job Misato did at raising Shinji. When I first saw Eva, I just took her words and actions at face value...but after seeing Evacommentary I realized just how badly she messed up, likely without even realizing it herself.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Mon Aug 24, 2009 8:06 am FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: The other thing I casually noticed during the first viewing was Anno's commentary on masks and how he carried that into a visual motif with clothing. The clothing the character's wear represented a "mask" or a wall that they were putting on for others (later it would be called the "AT Field"). Since the clothing represented the characters' "AT Field", the few times when you see the characters' true thought come out was when they were without clothing.

The clothing the character's wear represented a "mask" or a wall that they were putting on for others (later it would be called the "AT Field"). Since the clothing represented the characters' "AT Field", the few times when you see the characters' true thought come out was when they were without clothing.
